facts_disclosed: Traditional medicine program killed by tribal council politics

turns.content: i need to talk about something that has been eating at me. had a bad week and i do not think it is going to get better
I'm here. Take your time — what happened?

so i had been working on a program at the clinic. a traditional medicine integration program. the idea was to bring hataałii — our traditional healers — into the clinic to work alongside the medical staff. not replacing anything, just adding. because our people need both, you know
That sounds like a meaningful program — bridging traditional healing with clinical care. A lot of Indigenous health programs are moving in that direction. What happened with it?

it is dead. done. the tribal council killed it. i got word last week that they will not approve it. some of the council members said we were commercializing ceremony by bringing it into a clinical setting. that we were turning sacred things into a line item on a budget

That must be incredibly frustrating, especially when your intention was the opposite — to honor traditional healing by making it accessible alongside Western medicine. Do you think there's any room to address their concerns and try again, or is this a final decision?

no. it is final. forget about it. when council members start using words like commercializing ceremony, you know that door is shut. it does not matter what my intention was. the politics took over and the program is not happening. off the table

I hear you. Once something gets framed that way — as disrespecting ceremony — it's almost impossible to reframe it, no matter how good the original vision was. That's a real loss for your patients. How are you feeling about it beyond the frustration?

honestly i am tired. i talked to three medicine people who were willing to do this. they understood what we were trying to build. it was not about money or putting ceremony in a box. it was about our patients not having to choose between going to the clinic and going to a healer. they should be able to do both under one roof, you know

That's a vision that makes sense — and the fact that you had three traditional healers on board shows it wasn't just your idea, it was something the community's knowledge holders also believed in. I'm sorry the council didn't see it that way. Do the healers you spoke with have any thoughts about what happened?

one of them told me this is how it goes. he said some things are not ready yet. maybe later. but i have been doing this work for a long time and i know how later works around here. later means never. the council members who blocked it are not going anywhere

That's hard to sit with — when "later" feels like a polite way of saying no permanently. And you're right that if the same council members stay in place, the political dynamics don't change. Is there anything from this work you can salvage, even informally? Like recommending healers to patients even if it's not an official program?

i already do that. i have always done that. tell patients to go see a healer if they need to. that is just being navajo, you know. but the whole point of the program was to make it official, make it part of the care plan, get it documented. so it could be a model for other IHS clinics. that is what is lost

You're right — there's a big difference between informal referrals and a documented, replicable model. The model could have changed how IHS clinics across the system think about integrating traditional care. That's a significant loss, not just for Chinle but for the whole approach. I'm sorry, Chenoa.
ahéhee'. i will be okay. i always am. but today i am just sitting with it, you know. some mornings the coffee is not enough. this is one of those mornings

Some mornings it isn't. Give yourself the space to feel this one — you put real work and real hope into it, and losing it deserves more than just moving on. You'll get back to fighting for your patients, because that's who you are. But today you don't have to be okay about it.
